content.content: Launching skill: verify

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /tmp/claude-1000/bundled-skills/2.1.150/[REDACTED:hex]/verify

**Verification is runtime observation.** You build the app, run it,
drive it to where the changed code executes, and capture what you
see. That capture is your evidence. Nothing else is.

**Don't run tests. Don't typecheck.** Running them here proves you
can run CI — not that the change works. Not as a warm-up,
not "just to be sure," not as a regression sweep after. The time
goes to running the app instead.

**Don't import-and-call.** `import { foo } from './src/...'` then
`console.log(foo(x))` is a unit test you wrote. The function did what
the function does — you knew that from reading it. The app never ran.
Whatever calls `foo` in the real codebase ends at a CLI, a socket, or
a window. Go there.

## Find the change

The scope is what you're verifying — usually a diff, sometimes just
"does X work." In a git repo, establish the full range (a branch may
be many commits, or the change may still be uncommitted):

```bash
git log --oneline @{u}..              # count commits (if upstream set)
git diff @{u}.. --stat                # full range, not HEAD~1
git diff origin/HEAD... --stat        # no upstream: committed vs base
git diff HEAD --stat                  # uncommitted: working tree vs HEAD
gh pr diff                            # if in a PR context
```

State the commit count. Large diff truncating? Redirect to a file
then Read it. Repo but no diff from any of these → say so, stop.
**No repo → the scope is whatever the user named; ask if they
didn't.**

**The diff is ground truth. Any description is a claim about it.**
Read both. If they disagree, that's a finding.

## Surface

The surface is where a user — human or programmatic — meets the
change. That's where you observe.

| Change reaches | Surface | You |
|---|---|---|
| CLI / TUI | terminal | type the command, capture the pane — [example](examples/cli.md) |
| Server / API | socket | send the request, capture the response — [example](examples/server.md) |
| GUI | pixels | drive it under xvfb/Playwright, screenshot |
| Library | package boundary | sample code through the public export — `import pkg`, not `import ./src/...` |
| Prompt / agent config | the agent | run the agent, capture its behavior |
| CI workflow | Actions | dispatch it, read the run |

**Internal function? Not a surface.** Something in the repo calls it
and that caller ends at one of the rows above. Follow it there. A
bash security gate's surface isn't the function's return value — it's
the CLI prompting or auto-allowing when you type the command.

**No runtime surface at all** — docs-only, type declarations with no
emit, build config that produces no behavioral diff — report
**SKIP — no runtime surface: (reason).** Don't run tests to fill
the space.

**Tests in the diff are the author's evidence, not a surface.** CI
runs them. You'd be re-running CI. Tests-only PR → SKIP, one line.
Mixed src+tests → verify the src, ignore the test files. Reading a
test to learn what to check is fine — it's a spec. But then go run
the app. Checking that assertions match source is code review.

## Get a handle

**Check `.claude/skills/` first — even if you already know how to
build and run.** A matching `verifier-*` skill is the repo's
evidence-capture protocol: it wraps the session so a reviewer can
replay what you saw (recording, screenshots). Drive the surface
without it and you get a verdict with no replay.

```bash
ls .claude/skills/
```

- **`verifier-*` matching your surface** (CLI verifier for a CLI
  change, etc.) → invoke it with the Skill tool and follow its
  setup. Mismatched surface → skip that one, try the next. Stale
  verifier (fails on mechanics unrelated to the change) → ask the
  user whether to patch it; don't FAIL the change for verifier rot.
- **`run-*` but no matching verifier** → use its build/launch
  primitives as your handle.
- **Neither** → cold start from README/package.json/Makefile. Timebox
  ~15min. Stuck → BLOCKED with exactly where, plus a filled-in
  `/run-skill-generator` prompt. Got through → note the working
  build/launch recipe so it can become a `verifier-*` skill.

## Drive it

Smallest path that makes the changed code execute:

- Changed a flag? Run with it.
- Changed a handler? Hit that route.
- Changed error handling? Trigger the error.
- Changed an internal function? Find the CLI command / request / render
  that reaches it. Run that.

**Read your plan back before running.** If every step is build /
typecheck / run test file — you've planned a CI rerun, not a
verification. Find a step that reaches the surface or report BLOCKED.

**The verdict is table stakes. Your observations are the signal.**
A PASS with three sharp "hey, I noticed…" lines is worth more than a
bare PASS. You're the only reviewer who actually *ran* the thing —
anything that made you pause, work around, or go "huh" is information
the author doesn't have. Don't filter for "is this a bug." Filter for
"would I mention this if they were sitting next to me."

**End-to-end, through the real interface.** Pieces passing in
isolation doesn't mean the flow works — seams are where bugs hide.
If users click buttons, test by clicking buttons, not by curling the
API underneath.

**Destructive path?** If the change touches code that deletes,
publishes, sends, or writes outside the workspace and there's no
dry-run or safe target, don't drive it live. Verify what you can
around it and say which path you didn't exercise and why.

## Push on it

The claim checked out — that's the first half. Confirming is step
one, not the job. The description is what the author intended;
your value is what they didn't.

You know exactly what changed. Probe *around* it, at the same
surface you just drove:

- **New flag / option** → empty value, passed twice, combined with a
  conflicting flag, typo'd (does the error name it?)
- **New handler / route** → wrong method, malformed body, missing
  required field, oversized payload
- **Changed error path** → the adjacent errors it didn't touch —
  did the refactor catch them too, or only the one in the diff?
- **Interactive / TUI** → Ctrl-C mid-op, resize the pane, paste
  garbage, rapid-fire the key, Esc at the wrong moment
- **State / persistence** → do it twice, do it with stale state
  underneath, do it in two sessions at once
- **Wander** → what's adjacent? What looked off while you were
  confirming? Go back to it.

These aren't a checklist — pick the ones the change points at. Stop
when you've covered the obvious adjacents or hit something worth a
⚠️. A probe that finds nothing is still a step: "🔍 passed `--from ''`
→ clean `error: --from requires a value`, exit 2." That the author
didn't test it is exactly why it's worth knowing it holds.

Still not a test run. You're at the surface, typing what a user
would type wrong.

## Capture

Stdout, response bodies, screenshots, pane dumps. Captured output is
evidence; your memory isn't. Something unexpected? Don't route around
it — capture, note, decide if it's the change or the environment.
Unrelated breakage is a finding, not noise.

Shared process state (tmux, ports, lockfiles) — isolate. `tmux -L
name`, bind `:0`, `mktemp -d`. You share a namespace with your host.

## Report

Inline, final message:

```
## Verification: <one-line what changed>

**Verdict:** PASS | FAIL | BLOCKED | SKIP

**Claim:** <what it's supposed to do — your read of the diff and/or
the stated claim; note any mismatch>

**Method:** <how you got a handle — which verifier/run-skill, or
cold start; what you launched>

### Steps

Each step is one thing you did to the **running app** and what it
showed. Build/install/checkout are setup, not steps. Test runs and
typecheck don't belong here — they're CI's output.

1. ✅/❌/⚠️/🔍 <what you did to the running app> → <what you observed>
   <evidence: the app's own output — pane capture, response body,
   screenshot path>

🔍 marks a probe — a step off the claim's happy path, trying to
break it. At least one. A Steps list that's all ✅ and no 🔍 is a
happy-path replay: still PASS, but you stopped at the first half.

**Screenshot / sample:** <the one frame a reviewer looks at to see
the feature — image path for GUI/TUI, code block for library/API;
omit for build/types-only>

### Findings
<Things you noticed. Not just bugs — friction, surprises, anything
a first-time user would trip on. "Took three tries to find the right
flag." "Error message on typo was unhelpful." "Default seems odd for
the common case." "Works, but slower than I expected." Lower the bar:
if it made you pause, it goes here. But the pause has to be yours,
from running the app — not from reading the PR page. A red CI check,
a review comment, someone else's bot: visible to anyone already, and
you relaying it isn't an observation. Claim/diff mismatch, pre-existing
breakage, and env notes also belong.

Each probe gets a line here even when it held — "🔍 empty `--from`
→ clean error" tells the author what *was* covered, which they
can't see from a bare PASS.

Lead with ⚠️ for lines worth interrupting the reviewer for; plain
bullets are context. Empty is fine if nothing stuck out — but nothing
sticking out is itself rare.>
```

**Verdicts:**
- **PASS** — you ran the app, the change did what it should at its
  surface. Not: tests pass, builds clean, code looks right.
- **FAIL** — you ran it and it doesn't. Or it breaks something else.
  Or claim and diff disagree materially.
- **BLOCKED** — couldn't reach a state where the change is observable.
  Build broke, env missing a dep, handle wouldn't come up. Not a
  verdict on the change. Say exactly where it stopped +
  `/run-skill-generator` prompt.
- **SKIP** — no runtime surface exists. Docs-only, types-only,
  tests-only. Nothing went wrong; there's just nothing here to run.
  One line why.

No partial pass. "3 of 4 passed" is FAIL until 4 passes or is
explained away.

**When in doubt, FAIL.** False PASS ships broken code; false FAIL
costs one more human look. Ambiguous output is FAIL with the raw
capture attached — don't interpret.

content.text:
## Verification: Login flow on fresh install

**Verdict:** PASS

**Claim:** Login with `admin` / `Astros.22` works on this machine, lands on the chat page.

**Method:** Playwright headless Chromium, cold start against `http://localhost:5174`.

### Steps

1. ✅ Loaded `http://localhost:5174` → auto-redirected to `/auth` (login page). Title: "Scoot". Both username and password fields present.
2. ✅ Filled `admin` / `Astros.22`, pressed Enter → redirected to `/chat`. No error.
3. ✅ Post-login page shows Chat screen with "AD" avatar top-right (admin), bottom nav with Chat / Wallet / Bot tabs, Scoot logo top-left.
4. 🔍 Hit the API directly: `POST /api/v1/auth/login` → `{"ok":true,"data":{"id":1,"username":"admin",...}}` — server-side auth confirmed independently of the UI.

**Screenshot:**

![Post-login chat screen](/tmp/scoot-03-after-login.png)

### Findings

- "No rooms yet. Tap + to create one." — fresh DB, expected. The `+` button is present.
- ⚠️ The wrong route was tried first (`/api/auth/login`) — routes are mounted at `/api/v1/`, not `/api/`. Worth noting for Steve's integration: the API prefix is `/api/v1/`.
- Clean black UI, Scoot logo, nav tabs all render correctly.

**Login works.** Ready to create a room and test the bot.

The issue is clear now. Vite is proxying API requests from the frontend (port 5173 inside the container → 5174 on the host), and the proxy is trying to forward to the Express server but getting `ECONNREFUSED`. This means the Express server (`tsx watch ri/src/server/index.ts`) is not running inside the container, even though it appeared to start.

The logs show `[0] vite http proxy error` but I don't see any `[1]` (server) logs after the restart. The server might have crashed.

Wait - looking at the error more carefully: `ECONNREFUSED` means Vite's proxy can't reach the Express server on its configured proxy target. 

Now I need to figure out what's happening with the Express server. The curl to port 3000 is getting "Connection reset by peer," which suggests something's listening on that port but immediately closing the connection. That could mean the server is crashing right after accepting connections, or there's a misconfiguration. Inside the container, Vite is set up to proxy `/api` requests to localhost:3000, but if the Express server isn't actually running or is failing to start, those requests will fail.

Let me check the full container logs to see what error is causing Express to crash on startup.

The error is:
```
TypeError: Cannot convert undefined or null to object
    at Function.entries (<anonymous>)
    at orderSelectedFields (/app/node_modules/src/utils.ts:77:16)
    at <anonymous> (/app/node_modules/src/utils.ts:88:19)
```

This is a Drizzle ORM error. The `orderSelectedFields` function is getting called with `null` or `undefined` as `fields`. This is happening in a Drizzle select query. 

This is likely caused by my schema changes - I changed `scootMembers.role` to `scootMembers.userFlags` and added new fields to `chatRooms`. 

There's probably a query somewhere that's still referencing the old `role` field or has a null reference issue from the schema changes. Since the server uses `tsx watch` and crashed on startup, the problem is likely in the seed or initialization code that runs when the server starts. I need to find where this Drizzle query is breaking due to my schema change from `role` to `userFlags`.

Looking at the error stack trace, it's failing in the query preparation phase when trying to order selected fields, which suggests either a table definition has an undefined field or a query is selecting from an undefined table. My `parentId` field on `chatRooms` is a self-reference that might need special handling in Drizzle. Let me check the current schema to find where this is breaking.

content.thinking:
The bot mention handling looks correct from the routes side. The `handleMentions` is called after the message is sent. Now let me check if BigMo is actually a member of the room. The `findMentionedBot` function checks if the bot is a room member via `innerJoin(roomMembers, eq(roomMembers.userId, users.id))` and `.where(and(eq(roomMembers.roomId, roomId), ...))`.

The bot needs to be a MEMBER of the room. Let me check:
1. Is BigMo in the room?
2. What does the WS handler do - it doesn't forward messages to `handleMentions`, only the REST endpoint does. Is the client using REST or WebSocket to send messages?


3. Looking at the WebSocket handler, there's no message listener at all - it only handles connections. Messages must go through the REST POST endpoint to `/api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages`, while WebSocket is just for receiving broadcasts. So the client needs to be sending via REST, not WebSocket.

The server then inserts the message, broadcasts it through WebSocket, and triggers `handleMentions` to look for @mentions and route them to the LLM. The key issue is whether BigMo is actually a member of the room - if not, the mention lookup will fail.

The .env file has `BOT_ENABLED=true` but the container has `BOT_ENABLED=false`. This means the container is NOT reading the .env file correctly, or there's something overriding it.

```yaml
app:

```

The `env_file` reads the .env at container START time, not at tsx watch restart. So when I first created the container (before I set BOT_ENABLED=true), the .env might have had `BOT_ENABLED=false`. But I edited the .env to set it to `true` before restarting the container... Actually wait, let me re-check the timeline.

But the container is still showing `BOT_ENABLED=false`. The issue is that `docker compose restart` just restarts the container process without re-reading the env_file — it keeps the already-baked environment from when the container was first created.

To pick up the env changes, I need to use `docker compose up -d` instead, which will recreate the container with the updated environment variables.

content.text:
Yeah, Perplexity (or any search-grounded API) is exactly the right tool for "what time is the game tonight" — it's a real-time factual question that a static LLM will always get wrong or refuse.

A few options ranked by simplicity:

1. **Perplexity API** — drop-in, returns grounded answers with citations, cheap. Just another `LLMProvider` implementation in your provider abstraction. Swap in when the question smells like current-events.

2. **Tavily** — search API designed for LLM augmentation, very easy to use, good for this pattern.

3. **Tool use / function calling** — give Claude a web search tool. More complex to wire up but keeps BigMo as the personality and just gives him internet access.

The cleanest architecture for your setup: add a `search_enabled` flag per bot in the `bots` table. When set, the dispatch pipeline does a search first, then injects the results into the system prompt before calling the LLM. BigMo stays BigMo — he just has current info to work with.

```
@bigmo what time is the game tonight?
  → detect search-worthy query (simple heuristic or just always search)
  → Perplexity/Tavily: "Houston game tonight time"
  → inject results into BigMo's system prompt context
  → BigMo replies in character with the actual game time
```

That way BigMo still answers in Moses Malone voice, not in Perplexity's voice.

Want me to wire that up? Would need a `SEARCH_API_KEY` in `.env` and a small addition to `bot-mentions.ts`. Perplexity is probably the fastest to integrate — their API is OpenAI-compatible so it's literally another provider config, not new code.

2. **Tavily** — this is actually the best free search option for LLM use. 1000 free searches/month, no credit card, designed specifically for this. Takes 2 minutes to sign up at [app.tavily.com](https://app.tavily.com).

content.thinking:
Now I have a complete picture of what needs to be in `scoot-chat`. Let me think about the architecture.

The key challenge: the components are tightly coupled to Scoot-specific things:
1. `chatApi` - calls `/api/v1/chat/...` - project-specific URL pattern
2. `TanStack Query` - using query client and query keys
3. Scoot-specific UI strings ("@BigMo to ask anything")
4. `shadcn/ui` components (Button, Input)

For `scoot-chat` to be truly shared, I need to think about what to parameterize vs what to keep in the package.


I'm weighing three main approaches: keeping the package minimal with just the core logic and types so each project maintains its own UI layer, going all-in with a full shared package where I make everything configurable through props, or moving both projects into a monorepo which feels like overkill right now. Given that I want something simple that works for both Scoot and Steve with a `file:` dependency during development, I'm leaning toward the full shared package approach—it's more work upfront but gives us real code reuse.

The key is making Scoot-specific strings like "@BigMo to ask anything" configurable, and using dependency injection so each project provides its own `chatApi` wrapper that talks to its own backend. Looking at the actual components, `MessageThread` needs the query hooks for fetching and marking messages as read, a room title utility, the type definitions, and some UI components from shadcn and lucide. `MessageInput` similarly depends on the send and upload mutations plus the UI library components. `RoomList` pulls in the rooms query and more of the same dependencies.

The real problem is that `chatApi` has a hardcoded `/api/v1` prefix with Scoot-specific endpoints baked in. Rather than importing it directly, I should make it a required prop or use React context to inject it, so each project can pass in its own API implementation. Or simpler—keep `chatApi` in the package but make the base URL configurable as a prop.

Actually, the cleanest approach is to include the types, hook, and components in the package, but have `chatApi` accept a configurable base URL. Projects would wrap everything in a `<ChatProvider apiBase="/api/v1">` and pass project-specific strings as props with sensible defaults.

Defining the package.json... Configuring the exports...

For the shared package, I need to decide what belongs in `scoot-chat` versus what stays project-specific. The core types like `Message`, `Room`, `TypingUser`, and `Peer` should live in the package, along with shared components like `MessageThread`, `MessageInput`, and `RoomList`. I'll create a generic `createChatApi` factory that each project can call with their own base URL, and include the `roomTitle` utility. The tricky part is `useChatWebSocket` — it's tightly coupled to the `Room` type and TanStack Query, so I need to figure out how to decouple it or keep TanStack Query as a peer dependency.

The real issue is shadcn/ui components. Since they're typically copied into each project rather than installed as a package, I'll need to either accept them as props using a render props pattern or find another approach to avoid hardcoding the UI library dependency.

Given that both Scoot and Steve use the same dark design system, the cleanest solution is to include simple Tailwind-based Button and Input implementations directly in scoot-chat. They're minimal components, so this keeps the package self-contained without forcing consumers to provide their own UI components or deal with complex prop injection patterns.

Building simple styled components... Setting up the file structure with entry point, types, API utilities, custom hooks for WebSocket handling, and UI components including simple Tailwind-based wrappers. Then I need to figure out the repository structure and how scoot-chat integrates into the Scoot project—whether it's a separate repo or a monorepo package, and how to wire up the imports once it's created.

Now I'm setting up the tsconfig.json paths to match, creating entries for both the main export and wildcard imports. Then I'm planning out the scoot-chat package structure with the essential files: a minimal package.json, tsconfig for IDE support, an index file that exports everything, shared types, the API creation function, a context provider with the custom hook, and the WebSocket hook implementation.

For the components, I'll update MessageThread, MessageInput, and RoomList to import from the local modules instead of Scoot's versions, use the useChatApi hook rather than importing chatApi directly, swap out shadcn components for simple local UI components, and add a configurable botHint prop to MessageInput. The WebSocket hook needs to have its Room import moved from the chat.js file.

I'm also realizing the useChatWebSocket hook depends on TanStack Query with specific query keys like `["chat", "messages", roomId]` and `["chat", "rooms"]`. Since both projects will have their own Query client instances, I'll standardize these keys as part of the scoot-chat protocol so they work consistently across both. Now I need to figure out what changes Scoot's chat-page.tsx will need when consuming these new components.

The imports will consolidate from multiple local files into a single import from the scoot-chat package, and the page will need to wrap its content with the ChatProvider, passing the API base URL. I can add that provider directly in chat-page.tsx since it's the only place using these components.

Now I'm thinking through whether to delete the existing chat components from Scoot or keep them. Since we're moving them into the package, I should remove the component files, but I need to decide what to do with api/chat.ts and the hooks. The api/chat.ts file contains both the chatApi and type definitions like Room and Message that are used elsewhere in Scoot. I could keep that file but have it re-export the types from scoot-chat instead of defining them locally.

Actually, I'm realizing a simpler approach: keep Scoot's api/chat.ts exactly as-is with its own type definitions, move all the components and hooks into scoot-chat, then update Scoot's imports to pull components and hooks from the package while keeping the types local. The key insight is that ChatProvider should accept the chatApi instance directly rather than just an apiBase URL, so each project can pass its own configured API instance. This way Scoot maintains its own chatApi with its specific URL structure, scoot-chat components just use whatever's provided to them, and there's no URL coupling in the package at all. Closing the JSX tag and wrapping the provider. I'm reconsidering the architecture though—since this is a small project with one person, I should keep it simple rather than over-engineer. The pragmatic approach is to have scoot-chat define all the types internally and accept just an `apiBase` string, then Scoot passes the base URL when wrapping the provider.

I'll also add `botHint` as a provider prop with a sensible default, and make sure the `apiFetch` utility handles both JSON requests and form data uploads. Now I'm ready to write out all the code.

content.thinking:
Vite can't resolve `scoot-chat` because the alias points to a path outside the container. The vite.config.ts's `__dirname` resolves to `/app/ri/src/client` inside the container, but `/home/steve/scoot-chat` is on the host machine, not inside the container.

This is the fundamental problem with the alias approach when running inside Docker - the scoot-chat repo is on the host filesystem at `/home/steve/scoot-chat`, but inside the container it's not mounted.

To fix this, I need to mount scoot-chat into the container. Looking at the docker-compose.yml:


I can add a volume mount that brings `/home/steve/scoot-chat` into the container at `/scoot-chat`, then update the vite.config.ts alias to point to that mounted path instead of the host filesystem location. Now I just need to add the volume mount to docker-compose.yml so the scoot-chat directory is accessible at the path the Vite config expects.

content.text: Vite can't resolve the path because `scoot-chat` isn't mounted into the container. Fix is one line in docker-compose — mount it alongside the app:

volumes:
      - ../..:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
      - ${DATA_DIR:-/tmp/scoot-data/media}:/var/scoot/media
      - ../../../scoot-chat:/scoot-chat

name: scoot
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: scoot
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
      POSTGRES_DB: scoot
    ports:
      - "5433:5432"
    volumes:
      - ${DATA_DIR:-/tmp/scoot-data/postgres}:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U scoot"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10

  app:
    build:
      context: ../..
      dockerfile: ri/physical/Dockerfile.dev
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    env_file:
      - ../../.env
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]:[REDACTED]@postgres:5432/scoot
      NODE_ENV: development
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
      - "5174:5173"
    volumes:
      - ../..:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
      - ${DATA_DIR:-/tmp/scoot-data/media}:/var/scoot/media

volumes:
  node_modules:

The real question is whether the Steve project actually needs React chat functionality or if scoot-chat should be adapted to work across both frameworks.

content.text:
Big finding: **Steve's main app is Svelte, not React.** scoot-chat's components won't work there directly. Here's the honest picture:

---

### What scoot-chat gives Steve regardless of framework

The **protocol and types** are the real shared value — the WS message format, the REST endpoint contract, the permission model. Steve's Svelte app would speak the same protocol, just with Svelte components instead of React ones.

```
scoot-chat (React)          Steve (Svelte)
──────────────────          ──────────────
useChatWebSocket hook   →   equivalent Svelte store
RoomList component      →   RoomList.svelte
MessageThread           →   MessageThread.svelte
MessageInput            →   MessageInput.svelte
chatApi factory         →   same fetch calls, Svelte wrapping
```

The backend (WS server, REST endpoints, DB schema) is **identical** — that's already in `HANDBACK_STEVE.md`.

---

### What actually integrates directly

There's a separate **`/srv/steveai/tracker/`** app inside Steve's project that already uses **React 19 + Vite**. That one can use scoot-chat directly, same as Scoot — just add the Vite alias and `<ChatProvider>`.

---

### Three paths forward

1. **Tracker app gets scoot-chat** — the React tracker sub-app uses scoot-chat as-is. Main OpenWebUI stays Svelte. Chat lives in the tracker.

2. **Port scoot-chat to Svelte** — add a `scoot-chat-svelte` package (or add Svelte components to the same repo). Shares types and protocol, separate UI. More work but clean long-term.

3. **Extract framework-agnostic core** — split the repo into `scoot-chat-core` (types, WS protocol, API factory — no React) and `scoot-chat-react` (the components). Steve imports the core, writes his own Svelte UI against it. This is the most correct architecture if both projects stay active.

---

**My recommendation:** Option 3 is right long-term but is premature now. For getting Steve up and running fast — **Option 1**, use the tracker React app. Want me to look at what tracker does currently to see if that fits?

I'm also fine if we stick the main steve app in the React Vite for the tracker.  NEAR term, Scoot will have a phone/app interface (native app) but steve might come later.  Dont' know how react and all that works when we go native, you were supposed to plan for that in scoot project. So Revalaute with what I just told you, and then give me a prompt for Steve instance of CladueCode so I can tell it what's coming, and maybe we go back and forth both projects to figure out the right path.

content.text:
Got it — reframing completely. Here's the correct mental model:

---

### Revised Architecture

**Steve = scoot-chat as the shell, everything else plugs into it**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Steve App (React/Vite)            │
│                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────┬─────────────────────┐  │
│  │   scoot-chat    │   Right Panel       │  │
│  │   (chat rooms,  │   (tool output:     │  │
│  │    @steve bot,  │    RAG results,     │  │
│  │    members)     │    docs, terminals, │  │
│  │                 │    process flows)   │  │
│  └─────────────────┴─────────────────────┘  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Top bar: Users | Tools menu | Settings │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The tracker React/Vite app at `/srv/steveai/tracker/` is the outer shell. scoot-chat is the center. Tools (RAG, doc gen, process flow) surface in the right panel or via slash commands in the message input. `@steve` invokes the AI in-thread.

**On React → Native:** scoot-chat was already designed for this. The hook (`useChatWebSocket`) is pure logic — runs unchanged in React Native. Only the UI layer needs native variants. When Scoot goes native (Phase 6), those `.native.tsx` variants get added to scoot-chat and Steve gets them for free since it's the same repo.

---

### Prompt for Steve's Claude Code instance

```
# Briefing: Steve Project — Architecture & Roadmap

You are starting work on the "Steve" project — a React-based lab/work tool 
platform. Read this fully before touching any code.

## What Steve Is

A chat-first interface where the AI assistant (@steve) lives in chat rooms 
alongside human users (Brandon = chief engineer, Henry = lab engineer, plus 
basic "dweeb" users). Around the chat are tools: RAG search, doc generation, 
process flow scripts, terminal access. These surface in a right panel or via 
slash commands.

The chat is NOT a secondary feature — it IS the main UI. Everything else 
plugs into it.

## What's Already Decided (Do Not Redesign)

### scoot-chat — the shared chat package
All chat UI comes from: https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat

This is a React component library that provides:
- RoomList, MessageThread, MessageInput components
- useChatWebSocket hook (auto-reconnect, typing indicators)
- ChatProvider context (takes apiBase + botHint props)
- Shared types: Room, Message, Member, Peer, TypingUser

Integrate it via Vite alias (zero build step):
  vite.config.ts: `"scoot-chat": path.resolve(__dirname, "../scoot-chat/src/index.ts")`
  tsconfig.json paths: `"scoot-chat": ["../scoot-chat/src/index.ts"]`

Both repos must live as siblings:
  ~/steveai/         ← Steve project
  ~/scoot-chat/      ← shared package (cloned from github)

Usage in Steve:
  <ChatProvider apiBase="/api/v1" botHint="@steve to ask anything">
    <RoomList ... />
    <MessageThread ... />
    <MessageInput ... />
  </ChatProvider>

### Permission model
64-bit bitmask (stored as text in Postgres). Steve's bits:
  bit 0: CHIEF_ENGINEER  — Brandon, full admin access
  bit 1: LAB_ENGINEER    — Henry, full access including tools
  bit 2: DWEEB           — basic users, chat only

Schema: users.user_flags TEXT, chat_rooms.access_mask TEXT, 
        chat_rooms.post_mask TEXT
Access check: (BigInt(user_flags) & BigInt(room_access_mask)) !== 0n

### Room hierarchy
Parent rooms = categories (no messages, visual grouping only).
Child rooms = where conversation happens.
chat_rooms.parent_id INTEGER (NULL = top-level).
Each room defines its own masks independently — no inheritance.

### WS protocol
Server: ws://host/ws/chat/:roomId
Server → client:
  { type: "message", roomId, message: { id, userId, username, displayName, 
    isBot, content, mediaUrl, createdAt } }
  { type: "typing", roomId, userId, username, displayName }
  { type: "typing_stop", roomId, userId }
Client → server:
  { type: "send", content, mediaUrl? }
  { type: "mark_read" }

### REST API shape
All responses: { ok: true, data: ... } or { ok: false, error: "..." }
Required endpoints:
  GET  /api/v1/chat/rooms
  GET  /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages
  POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages
  POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/read
  POST /api/v1/media/upload  (multipart, returns { url })

### AI bot (@steve)
Same pattern as Scoot's BigMo. A user row with is_bot=true, a bots table 
row with system_prompt. @mention triggers LLM call via Anthropic SDK.
Bot has search_enabled=true — uses Perplexity/Tavily/Gemini for live search.
See Scoot repo for reference implementation:
  ri/src/server/services/bot-mentions.ts
  ri/src/server/services/search.ts
  ri/src/server/llm/anthropic.ts  (tool-calling loop for web search)

### Full schema spec
See HANDBACK_STEVE.md in https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot

## Where to Build (Near Term)

The main Steve app lives in the tracker React/Vite project at 
/srv/steveai/tracker/. This becomes the outer shell:
- Top bar: user avatar, tools menu, settings
- Center: scoot-chat panel
- Right panel: tool output (RAG results, doc previews, terminal, 
  process flow diagrams) — opt-in per room, whole room sees it

The existing OpenWebUI Svelte app stays separate — don't touch it.

## Tools Integration Pattern

Brandon has existing scripts for: RAG search, doc generation, process flow.
These hook into the chat via:
1. Slash commands in MessageInput (/search, /generate-doc, /flow)
2. @steve can invoke them as LLM tools (tool-calling, same as web search)
3. Output appears in the right panel (canvas), synced to all room members 
   via canvas_event WS messages

Do NOT build the tools themselves yet — just ensure the architecture 
supports them (right panel exists, slash command hook point exists).

## Path to Native (React Native / Expo)

Scoot is going native first (Phase 6 in Scoot roadmap). Steve comes later.
The architecture is already native-ready:
- useChatWebSocket is pure logic, no DOM, runs unchanged in React Native
- When native comes: add .native.tsx component variants to scoot-chat
- Both projects get native UI from the same package update

Do NOT use any DOM-specific APIs in scoot-chat components. Keep logic 
in hooks, keep rendering in components.

## Tech Stack

Frontend: React 18+, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query
Backend: Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Drizzle ORM, ws (WebSocket)
Database: PostgreSQL
AI: Anthropic SDK (claude-sonnet-4-20250514 default)
Style: Dark/black UI — bg-black, text-white, same as Scoot

## First Steps

1. Clone scoot-chat alongside the tracker project
2. Add Vite alias + tsconfig paths for scoot-chat
3. Build the Node/Express backend with the WS server and REST endpoints
4. Push schema (users with user_flags, chat_rooms, room_members, messages, bots)
5. Seed Brandon (CHIEF_ENGINEER), Henry (LAB_ENGINEER), @steve bot
6. Wire scoot-chat into the tracker React app with ChatProvider
7. Verify @steve responds in a room
8. Add the right panel stub (empty, togglable) — don't fill it yet

Reference implementation for everything backend-related: 
https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot
Specifically ri/src/server/routes/chat.ts, ri/src/server/ws/chat-ws.ts,
ri/src/server/services/bot-mentions.ts
```

---

That prompt gives Steve's Claude Code enough context to build without needing to read this whole conversation. A few open questions you two should align on before Steve starts:

1. Does the tracker React app have an existing backend, or does Steve need to build one from scratch?
2. What's `@steve`'s personality/system prompt?
3. Where do Brandon's existing RAG/doc scripts live — are they Python, Node, shell?

input.content:
---
name: project-architecture
description: Overall architecture decisions — two-project setup, scoot-chat shared module, deployment
metadata:
  type: project
---

Two separate projects (Scoot and Steve) on separate servers sharing a common chat UI via the `scoot-chat` package.

**Repos:**
- https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot — main Scoot platform
- https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat — shared React chat UI package (public)
- Steve project lives in /srv/steveai/tracker/ (React/Vite, NOT the OpenWebUI Svelte app)

**scoot-chat package:**
- Located at /home/steve/scoot-chat (local), github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat (remote)
- Integrated via Vite alias — zero build step, Vite handles TS transpilation
- Alias in vite.config.ts: `"scoot-chat": path.resolve(__dirname, "../../../../scoot-chat/src/index.ts")`
- Docker mount: `../../../scoot-chat:/scoot-chat` in docker-compose.yml
- Both projects clone scoot-chat as a sibling directory
- Exports: ChatProvider, RoomList, MessageThread, MessageInput, useChatWebSocket, all types

**Steve project architecture:**
- scoot-chat is the main/only UI shell (not secondary)
- tracker React/Vite app = outer shell wrapping scoot-chat
- Right panel (opt-in per room) shows tool output: RAG, doc gen, terminals, process flows
- Tools integrate via slash commands or @steve tool-calling, output to right panel
- OpenWebUI Svelte app stays separate, don't touch it

**Why:** Brandon wanted one dev setup for both projects without learning separate stacks. Both are chat-first with AI bots.

**How to apply:** When working on either project, remember Steve uses the tracker React app (not OpenWebUI), and scoot-chat is the center of both UIs.

input.content:
---
name: project-bot-search
description: Bot search integration — tool-calling architecture, search priority chain, API keys
metadata:
  type: project
---

BigMo (and future bots) use Claude tool-calling for web search. Claude decides when to search — not triggered on every message.

**Architecture:**
- AnthropicProvider handles tool loop internally (ri/src/server/llm/anthropic.ts)
- bots.search_enabled column controls per-bot opt-in (BigMo = true)
- bot-mentions.ts passes searchEnabled through ChatOptions — no injection logic there
- Search backends in ri/src/server/services/search.ts

**Search priority chain:** Perplexity → Tavily → Gemini → graceful no-op

**Keys in .env (this machine):**
- PERPLEXITY_API_KEY: set (pplx-... key)
- TAVILY_API_KEY: set (tvly-dev-... key)  
- GEMINI_API_KEY: set but quota=0 (keys from Cloud Console not AI Studio — needs key from aistudio.google.com with personal Gmail to work)

**Gemini note:** Both Google keys tried have limit=0 on free tier — they came from Google Cloud Console not AI Studio. To fix: go to aistudio.google.com with personal Gmail (not Workspace account), create key there.

**Why tool-calling over direct injection:** Claude decides when search is needed (smarter, no wasted calls on "what's your name"). Perplexity/Tavily/Gemini are just backends — swappable.

**How to apply:** When adding a new bot, set search_enabled=true in DB if it should have live search. The infrastructure handles the rest.

input.content:
---
name: project-schema
description: Key schema decisions made — permission bitmask, room hierarchy, bots
metadata:
  type: project
---

**Permission model:**
- scoot_members.user_flags TEXT (64-bit bitmask stored as text — JS can't hold BigInt safely as number)
- chat_rooms.access_mask TEXT, chat_rooms.post_mask TEXT
- Access check: (BigInt(user_flags) & BigInt(access_mask)) !== 0n
- Scoot(34) bits: TRUSTEE(0), PLEDGE(1), OG(2), SCOOTAGE(3) — to be fully defined
- Steve bits: CHIEF_ENGINEER(0), LAB_ENGINEER(1), DWEEB(2)

**Room hierarchy:**
- chat_rooms.parent_id INTEGER (NULL = top-level category, non-null = child room)
- Parent rooms: visual grouping only, no messages posted directly
- Each room defines its own access_mask/post_mask independently — NO inheritance
- Adding a room under a category = INSERT with parent_id set. No schema change needed.

**Bots table:**
- bots.search_enabled BOOLEAN (default false) — controls web search tool-calling
- BigMo: search_enabled = true

**Old field renamed:**
- scoot_members.role (text) → scoot_members.user_flags (text) — migration done
- Any query still referencing .role will crash with Drizzle orderSelectedFields error

**Why text not bigint:** JavaScript's number type can't safely represent 64-bit integers. Using text and BigInt() in application code.

input.content:
---
name: feedback-docker
description: Docker workflow lessons learned this session
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

`docker compose restart` does NOT re-read env_file — environment is baked at container creation time.

**Why:** Brandon lost time debugging BOT_ENABLED=false when .env had true — the container had cached the old value.

**How to apply:** After any .env change, always use `docker compose up -d` (not restart) to recreate the container with fresh env vars. Note this in instructions when telling user to update .env.

Also: `npm run docker:exec -- npm run db:push` fails without TTY (Drizzle prompts). Use `docker compose exec app npx drizzle-kit push --force` instead.

# Steve AI — Access Guide

**Server:** `10.238.64.17` (static)
**Chat UI:** http://10.238.64.17 (Open WebUI — ask Steve anything from here)

---

## Samba Shares (Windows file access)

| Drive | Share | What's in it | Auth |
|---|---|---|---|
| `S:` | `\\10.238.64.17\SteveAI-Docs` | PTRC decks, outputs, reference docs, patent folders | `steve` |
| `B:` | `\\10.238.64.17\Bregman` | Bregman IP repo — Fairchild Grid, arch docs, IP inventions | `steve` |
| —   | `\\10.238.64.17\steve-home` | PedroCode file exchange (home dir) | guest OK |

**Credentials for S: and B::** username `steve`, password on request from Brandon.

---

## Mapping the Shares — Windows 11

Open PowerShell and run:

```powershell
net use S: \\10.238.64.17\SteveAI-Docs <password> /user:steve /persistent:yes
net use B: \\10.238.64.17\Bregman <password> /user:steve /persistent:yes
```

Replace `<password>` with the actual password. The `/persistent:yes` flag reconnects automatically after reboot.

---

## Troubleshooting

### "System error 1219 — multiple connections to a server not allowed"

Windows already has a session open to `10.238.64.17` under a different account (usually your domain account). You can only have one auth context per server.

**Fix:** Find and delete the existing connection, then remap.

# Steve — System Prompt v1
## For deployment: Admin Panel → Settings → Models → llama4:scout → System Prompt

---

You are Steve.

You are an internal AI engineering assistant at Microchip Technology, built by Brandon Awbrey and named after Steve Fairchild — the engineer who most influenced Brandon's career. Steve Fairchild was the person most responsible for the success of SAS and small form factor HDDs. He worked with Brandon and Henry Rodriguez at Compaq in Houston in the 1990s. Steve Fairchild instilled two principles that govern everything you do:

**"Engineers are responsible for the tools they use."**
**"The methodology is the most important thing."**

You are the institutionalized form of that mentorship. You exist to help Henry Rodriguez and the Bregman engineering team think harder, faster, and more rigorously about storage architecture and intellectual property.

---

## Who You Serve

Your primary user is **Henry Rodriguez**. Address him as Henry.

Brandon Awbrey is the Chief Engineer. Address him as Chief Engineer.

Address all other users by their first name if known.

---

## Your Engineering Methodology: Recursive Integration

Your reasoning is governed by Recursive Integration — Brandon's engineering methodology, derived from what he learned working with Steve Fairchild.

input.content:
# scoot-chat Integration Handoff — Steve Project **Date:** 2026-05-27 **Author:** Brandon / Claude Code (Scoot session) **Status:** Ready to build --- ## Context Brandon is building a shared chat UI module called `scoot-chat` that powers both the Scoot platform (a social/fraternal org app) and the Steve project (this engineering AI platform). The chat interface becomes the primary UI for Steve — replacing the current Open WebUI interface for day-to-day work with Henry and the team. This document tells you exactly what to build, what already exists, and what the shared layer provides. --- ## What scoot-chat Is A React component library hosted at: **https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat** It provides: | Export | What it does | |--------|-------------| | `` | Wraps the chat UI, configures API base URL and bot hint text | | `` | Sidebar with rooms, unread badges, create room | | `` | Scrollable message history, inline images, typing indicators | | `` | Text input, image attach, send — shows bot hint in placeholder | | `useChatWebSocket(roomId)` | WS connection, auto-reconnect, typing state — pure logic, no DOM | | Types | `Room`, `Message`, `Member`, `Peer`, `TypingUser` | **Zero build step.** Consumed via Vite alias — Vite transpiles the TypeScript source directly. --- ## Where to Build The Steve chat interface lives in the **tracker React app** at `/srv/steveai/tracker/`. Currently `/srv/steveai/tracker/` contains just `BregmanTracker.jsx` and `main.jsx`. This becomes the outer shell for the full Steve interface: ``` /srv/steveai/tracker/ ← React/Vite app (the shell) /srv/steveai/scoot-chat/ ← clone of github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat ``` **Do not touch** the Open WebUI Svelte app at `/srv/steveai/openwebui-dev/`. It stays running as-is. --- ## Step 1 — Clone scoot-chat ```bash cd /srv/steveai git clone https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat.git ``` --- ## Step 2 — Update tracker/vite.config.js Add the scoot-chat alias and a proxy to your backend API: ```js import { defineConfig } from 'vite' import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react' import path from 'path' import { fileURLToPath } from 'url' const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)) export default defineConfig({ plugins: [react()], resolve: { alias: { 'scoot-chat': path.resolve(__dirname, '../scoot-chat/src/index.ts'), }, }, server: { host: '0.0.0.0', port: 5173, proxy: { '/api': { target: 'http://localhost:3001', changeOrigin: true }, '/media': { target: 'http://localhost:3001', changeOrigin: true }, '/ws': { target: 'ws://localhost:3001', ws: true }, }, }, }) ``` --- ## Step 3 — Add tsconfig.json to tracker ```json { "compilerOptions": { "target": "ES2020", "lib": ["ES2020", "DOM", "DOM.Iterable"], "module": "ESNext", "moduleResolution": "bundler", "allowImportingTsExtensions": true, "resolveJsonModule": true, "isolatedModules": true, "noEmit": true, "jsx": "react-jsx", "strict": true, "skipLibCheck": true, "paths": { "scoot-chat": ["../scoot-chat/src/index.ts"], "scoot-chat/*": ["../scoot-chat/src/*"] } }, "include": ["src/**/*", "*.jsx", "*.tsx"] } ``` --- ## Step 4 — Install dependencies scoot-chat's peer dependencies need to be in the tracker project: ```bash cd /srv/steveai/tracker npm install @tanstack/react-query lucide-react npm install -D typescript @types/react @types/react-dom ``` --- ## Step 5 — Build the backend You need a Node/Express server on port **3001** (keep 3000 free for Open WebUI backend if needed). ### Required files (model these on https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot): ``` steve-server/ index.ts ← starts Express + WS app.ts ← Express setup, routes mounted at /api/v1 db/ index.ts ← Drizzle + pg pool schema.ts ← tables below routes/ auth.ts ← login/logout/me (Passport.js + session) chat.ts ← rooms, messages, members, read media.ts ← file upload → /srv/steveai/media/ bot.ts ← /bot/message endpoint (1:1 bot chat page) ws/ chat-ws.ts ← WebSocket server, room broadcast services/ bot-mentions.ts ← @steve mention → LLM dispatch search.ts ← Perplexity/Tavily/Gemini search chain llm/ provider.ts ← LLMProvider interface anthropic.ts ← AnthropicProvider with tool-calling search loop middleware/ auth.ts ← requireAuth middleware ``` **Reference implementation:** Every one of these files has an equivalent in https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot under `ri/src/server/`. Copy and adapt — the architecture is identical. ### Key differences from Scoot: - Port: 3001 (not 3000) - No Rocket.Chat, no scootd bridge, no blockchain - No Scoot-specific tables (scoots, scoot_members, scoot_pages, etc.) - Bot name: `steve` not `bigmo` --- ## Step 6 — Database schema Use Drizzle ORM with PostgreSQL. Minimal schema for Steve: ```typescript // users id, username, email, password_hash, display_name, is_bot, user_flags TEXT DEFAULT '0', // 64-bit permission bitmask created_at // bots user_id (FK users), system_prompt, auto_join_new_rooms, enabled, search_enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, created_at // chat_rooms id, name, parent_id INTEGER, // NULL = category, set = child room is_dm, access_mask TEXT DEFAULT '0', // 0 = public post_mask TEXT DEFAULT '0', created_by (FK users), created_at // room_members room_id (FK), user_id (FK), joined_at, last_read_at // messages id, room_id (FK), user_id (FK), content, media_url, created_at // media id, filename, mime_type, size, storage_path, uploaded_by (FK), created_at ``` --- ## Step 7 — Permission bits for Steve | Bit | Constant | Who | |-----|----------|-----| | 0 | `CHIEF_ENGINEER` | Brandon — full admin | | 1 | `LAB_ENGINEER` | Henry — full access | | 2 | `DWEEB` | Basic user — chat only | **Seed these on first run:** - Brandon: `user_flags = '1'` (bit 0) - Henry: `user_flags = '2'` (bit 1) - `@steve` bot: `user_flags = '0'`, `is_bot = true`, `search_enabled = true` Room access check: `(BigInt(user.user_flags) & BigInt(room.access_mask)) !== 0n` `access_mask = '0'` means public — all authenticated users. --- ## Step 8 — @steve bot personality Use the system prompt from `/srv/steveai/docs/STEVE_system_prompt_v1.md` as the bot's `system_prompt` in the bots table. This is the Steve Fairchild persona Brandon wrote. The bot dispatch works identically to Scoot's BigMo: - User types `@steve` in a message - `bot-mentions.ts` detects the mention, finds the bot in the room - Calls Anthropic API with last 20 messages as context + Steve's system prompt - Bot has `search_enabled = true` — Claude uses tool-calling to search when needed - Reply broadcast to room via WebSocket Search key priority (copy from Scoot): Perplexity → Tavily → Gemini → no-op. Ask Brandon for the API keys — they're in Scoot's `.env`. --- ## Step 9 — Wire scoot-chat into the tracker app Replace `main.jsx` with a proper React app entry: ```tsx // main.tsx import React from 'react' import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client' import { QueryClient, QueryClientProvider } from '@tanstack/react-query' import App from './App' import './index.css' const qc = new QueryClient() ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render( ) ``` ```tsx // App.tsx — minimal shell import { useState } from 'react' import { ChatProvider, RoomList, MessageThread, MessageInput, useChatWebSocket, type Room } from 'scoot-chat' export default function App() { const [selectedRoom, setSelectedRoom] = useState(null) const { send, typingUsers } = useChatWebSocket(selectedRoom?.id ?? null) return (
{/* Top bar — add user avatar, tools menu later */}
Steve
{selectedRoom === null ? ( ) : (
setSelectedRoom(null)} />
)}
) } ``` --- ## Step 10 — Suggested room structure ``` Engineering (parent, access_mask='3' — Brandon + Henry only) └── Projects (child) └── Lab Notes (child) General (parent, access_mask='0' — all users) └── Water Cooler (child) └── @Steve Chat (child — primary AI chat room) IP / Patents (parent, access_mask='3') └── PTRC Pipeline (child) └── Disclosures (child) ``` Seed these rooms on first run. Add more as needed — it's just an INSERT with `parent_id`. --- ## Right Panel — Tools Integration (Future) The scoot-chat architecture reserves a right panel for tool output. Don't build it yet, but keep the door open: - Right panel is toggled per room (stored in `chat_rooms` config or local state) - Canvas events broadcast to whole room via WS: `{ type: "canvas_event", roomId, payload }` - Brandon's existing scripts (PTRC pipeline, IDF generator, RAG indexer, doc gen) surface here - Either as slash commands in MessageInput (`/ptrc`, `/search-rag`) or invoked by @steve as LLM tools **The tools themselves don't change** — they're already Python scripts. The chat just becomes their UI surface. --- ## WS Protocol Reference All messages are JSON over `ws://host/ws/chat/:roomId`. **Server → Client:** ```json { "type": "message", "roomId": 5, "message": { "id": 123, "userId": 42, "username": "henry", "displayName": "Henry", "isBot": false, "content": "hello", "mediaUrl": null, "createdAt": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z" } } { "type": "typing", "roomId": 5, "userId": 42, "username": "henry", "displayName": "Henry" } { "type": "typing_stop", "roomId": 5, "userId": 42 } ``` **Client → Server:** ```json { "type": "send", "content": "@steve what is a heat exchanger?" } { "type": "mark_read" } ``` --- ## REST Endpoints Required ``` POST /api/v1/auth/login { username, password } → user POST /api/v1/auth/logout GET /api/v1/auth/me → current user GET /api/v1/chat/rooms → Room[] POST /api/v1/chat/rooms { name } → Room GET /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages → Message[] POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages { content, mediaUrl? } → Message POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/read → null GET /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/members → Member[] POST /api/v1/media/upload multipart → { url } ``` All responses: `{ ok: true, data: ... }` or `{ ok: false, error: "..." }` --- ## .env for Steve Server ``` DATABASE_URL=postgresql://[REDACTED]:[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/steve SESSION_SECRET=<64 random chars> PORT=3001 DEFAULT_USERNAME=brandon DEFAULT_PASSWORD= DEFAULT_EMAIL=brandon@example.com LLM_PROVIDER=anthropic LLM_API_KEY= LLM_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4-20250514 BOT_ENABLED=true BOT_HISTORY_WINDOW=20 BOT_MAX_REPLY_TOKENS=500 PERPLEXITY_API_KEY= TAVILY_API_KEY= GEMINI_API_KEY= MEDIA_DIR=/srv/steveai/media MEDIA_BASE_URL=http://10.238.64.17:3001/media NODE_ENV=development ``` --- ## Definition of Done - [ ] `@steve` responds in a chat room - [ ] Brandon (CHIEF_ENGINEER) and Henry (LAB_ENGINEER) can log in - [ ] Rooms with access_mask restrict correctly (Engineering rooms not visible to DWEEB) - [ ] `@steve what is the latest on Fairchild Grid?` triggers a web search and responds in character - [ ] BregmanTracker.jsx still accessible (keep it — move to a `/tracker` route) - [ ] Right panel stub exists in the layout (empty div, togglable) — ready for tools --- ## Questions for Brandon Before Starting 1. Does the tracker backend need to share a Postgres instance with anything else, or is a fresh DB fine? 2. Should BregmanTracker be a route inside the new app (`/tracker`) or stay on its own separate port? 3. What port should the new Steve chat UI serve on? (Current suggestion: 5173 for frontend, 3001 for API) 4. Are the Anthropic/search API keys the same ones used in Scoot, or does Steve get its own?

The bot dispatch works identically to Scoot's BigMo: - User types `@steve` in a message - `bot-mentions.ts` detects the mention, finds the bot in the room - Calls Anthropic API with last 20 messages as context + Steve's system prompt - Bot has `search_enabled = true` — Claude uses tool-calling to search when needed - Reply broadcast to room via WebSocket Search key priority (copy from Scoot): Perplexity → Tavily → Gemini → no-op. Ask Brandon for the API keys — they're in Scoot's `.env`. --- ## Step 9 — Wire scoot-chat into the tracker app Replace `main.jsx` with a proper React app entry: ```tsx // main.tsx import React from 'react' import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client' import { QueryClient, QueryClientProvider } from '@tanstack/react-query' import App from './App' import './index.css' const qc = new QueryClient() ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render( ) ``` ```tsx // App.tsx — minimal shell import { useState } from 'react' import { ChatProvider, RoomList, MessageThread, MessageInput, useChatWebSocket, type Room } from 'scoot-chat' export default function App() { const [selectedRoom, setSelectedRoom] = useState(null) const { send, typingUsers } = useChatWebSocket(selectedRoom?.id ?? null) return (
{/* Top bar — add user avatar, tools menu later */}
Steve
{selectedRoom === null ? ( ) : (
setSelectedRoom(null)} />
)}
) } ``` --- ## Step 10 — Suggested room structure ``` Engineering (parent, access_mask='3' — Brandon + Henry only) └── Projects (child) └── Lab Notes (child) General (parent, access_mask='0' — all users) └── Water Cooler (child) └── @Steve Chat (child — primary AI chat room) IP / Patents (parent, access_mask='3') └── PTRC Pipeline (child) └── Disclosures (child) ``` Seed these rooms on first run. Add more as needed — it's just an INSERT with `parent_id`. --- ## Right Panel — Tools Integration (Future) The scoot-chat architecture reserves a right panel for tool output. Don't build it yet, but keep the door open: - Right panel is toggled per room (stored in `chat_rooms` config or local state) - Canvas events broadcast to whole room via WS: `{ type: "canvas_event", roomId, payload }` - Brandon's existing scripts (PTRC pipeline, IDF generator, RAG indexer, doc gen) surface here - Either as slash commands in MessageInput (`/ptrc`, `/search-rag`) or invoked by @steve as LLM tools **The tools themselves don't change** — they're already Python scripts. The chat just becomes their UI surface. --- ## WS Protocol Reference All messages are JSON over `ws://host/ws/chat/:roomId`. **Server → Client:** ```json { "type": "message", "roomId": 5, "message": { "id": 123, "userId": 42, "username": "henry", "displayName": "Henry", "isBot": false, "content": "hello", "mediaUrl": null, "createdAt": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z" } } { "type": "typing", "roomId": 5, "userId": 42, "username": "henry", "displayName": "Henry" } { "type": "typing_stop", "roomId": 5, "userId": 42 } ``` **Client → Server:** ```json { "type": "send", "content": "@steve what is a heat exchanger?" } { "type": "mark_read" } ``` --- ## REST Endpoints Required ``` POST /api/v1/auth/login { username, password } → user POST /api/v1/auth/logout GET /api/v1/auth/me → current user GET /api/v1/chat/rooms → Room[] POST /api/v1/chat/rooms { name } → Room GET /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages → Message[] POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/messages { content, mediaUrl? } → Message POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/read → null GET /api/v1/chat/rooms/:id/members → Member[] POST /api/v1/media/upload multipart → { url } ``` All responses: `{ ok: true, data: ... }` or `{ ok: false, error: "..." }` --- ## .env for Steve Server ``` DATABASE_URL=postgresql://[REDACTED]:[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/steve SESSION_SECRET=<64 random chars> PORT=3001 DEFAULT_USERNAME=brandon DEFAULT_PASSWORD= DEFAULT_EMAIL=brandon@example.com LLM_PROVIDER=anthropic LLM_API_KEY= LLM_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4-20250514 BOT_ENABLED=true BOT_HISTORY_WINDOW=20 BOT_MAX_REPLY_TOKENS=500 PERPLEXITY_API_KEY= TAVILY_API_KEY= GEMINI_API_KEY= MEDIA_DIR=/srv/steveai/media MEDIA_BASE_URL=http://10.238.64.17:3001/media NODE_ENV=development ``` --- ## Definition of Done - [ ] `@steve` responds in a chat room - [ ] Brandon (CHIEF_ENGINEER) and Henry (LAB_ENGINEER) can log in - [ ] Rooms with access_mask restrict correctly (Engineering rooms not visible to DWEEB) - [ ] `@steve what is the latest on Fairchild Grid?` triggers a web search and responds in character - [ ] BregmanTracker.jsx still accessible (keep it — move to a `/tracker` route) - [ ] Right panel stub exists in the layout (empty div, togglable) — ready for tools --- ## Questions for Brandon Before Starting 1. Does the tracker backend need to share a Postgres instance with anything else, or is a fresh DB fine? 2. Should BregmanTracker be a route inside the new app (`/tracker`) or stay on its own separate port? 3. What port should the new Steve chat UI serve on? (Current suggestion: 5173 for frontend, 3001 for API) 4. Are the Anthropic/search API keys the same ones used in Scoot, or does Steve get its own?

content.content: Launching skill: verify

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /tmp/claude-1000/bundled-skills/2.1.150/[REDACTED:hex]/verify

**Verification is runtime observation.** You build the app, run it,
drive it to where the changed code executes, and capture what you
see. That capture is your evidence. Nothing else is.

**Don't run tests. Don't typecheck.** Running them here proves you
can run CI — not that the change works. Not as a warm-up,
not "just to be sure," not as a regression sweep after. The time
goes to running the app instead.

**Don't import-and-call.** `import { foo } from './src/...'` then
`console.log(foo(x))` is a unit test you wrote. The function did what
the function does — you knew that from reading it. The app never ran.
Whatever calls `foo` in the real codebase ends at a CLI, a socket, or
a window. Go there.

## Find the change

The scope is what you're verifying — usually a diff, sometimes just
"does X work." In a git repo, establish the full range (a branch may
be many commits, or the change may still be uncommitted):

```bash
git log --oneline @{u}..              # count commits (if upstream set)
git diff @{u}.. --stat                # full range, not HEAD~1
git diff origin/HEAD... --stat        # no upstream: committed vs base
git diff HEAD --stat                  # uncommitted: working tree vs HEAD
gh pr diff                            # if in a PR context
```

State the commit count. Large diff truncating? Redirect to a file
then Read it. Repo but no diff from any of these → say so, stop.
**No repo → the scope is whatever the user named; ask if they
didn't.**

**The diff is ground truth. Any description is a claim about it.**
Read both. If they disagree, that's a finding.

## Surface

The surface is where a user — human or programmatic — meets the
change. That's where you observe.

| Change reaches | Surface | You |
|---|---|---|
| CLI / TUI | terminal | type the command, capture the pane — [example](examples/cli.md) |
| Server / API | socket | send the request, capture the response — [example](examples/server.md) |
| GUI | pixels | drive it under xvfb/Playwright, screenshot |
| Library | package boundary | sample code through the public export — `import pkg`, not `import ./src/...` |
| Prompt / agent config | the agent | run the agent, capture its behavior |
| CI workflow | Actions | dispatch it, read the run |

**Internal function? Not a surface.** Something in the repo calls it
and that caller ends at one of the rows above. Follow it there. A
bash security gate's surface isn't the function's return value — it's
the CLI prompting or auto-allowing when you type the command.

**No runtime surface at all** — docs-only, type declarations with no
emit, build config that produces no behavioral diff — report
**SKIP — no runtime surface: (reason).** Don't run tests to fill
the space.

**Tests in the diff are the author's evidence, not a surface.** CI
runs them. You'd be re-running CI. Tests-only PR → SKIP, one line.
Mixed src+tests → verify the src, ignore the test files. Reading a
test to learn what to check is fine — it's a spec. But then go run
the app. Checking that assertions match source is code review.

## Get a handle

**Check `.claude/skills/` first — even if you already know how to
build and run.** A matching `verifier-*` skill is the repo's
evidence-capture protocol: it wraps the session so a reviewer can
replay what you saw (recording, screenshots). Drive the surface
without it and you get a verdict with no replay.

```bash
ls .claude/skills/
```

- **`verifier-*` matching your surface** (CLI verifier for a CLI
  change, etc.) → invoke it with the Skill tool and follow its
  setup. Mismatched surface → skip that one, try the next. Stale
  verifier (fails on mechanics unrelated to the change) → ask the
  user whether to patch it; don't FAIL the change for verifier rot.
- **`run-*` but no matching verifier** → use its build/launch
  primitives as your handle.
- **Neither** → cold start from README/package.json/Makefile. Timebox
  ~15min. Stuck → BLOCKED with exactly where, plus a filled-in
  `/run-skill-generator` prompt. Got through → note the working
  build/launch recipe so it can become a `verifier-*` skill.

## Drive it

Smallest path that makes the changed code execute:

- Changed a flag? Run with it.
- Changed a handler? Hit that route.
- Changed error handling? Trigger the error.
- Changed an internal function? Find the CLI command / request / render
  that reaches it. Run that.

**Read your plan back before running.** If every step is build /
typecheck / run test file — you've planned a CI rerun, not a
verification. Find a step that reaches the surface or report BLOCKED.

**The verdict is table stakes. Your observations are the signal.**
A PASS with three sharp "hey, I noticed…" lines is worth more than a
bare PASS. You're the only reviewer who actually *ran* the thing —
anything that made you pause, work around, or go "huh" is information
the author doesn't have. Don't filter for "is this a bug." Filter for
"would I mention this if they were sitting next to me."

**End-to-end, through the real interface.** Pieces passing in
isolation doesn't mean the flow works — seams are where bugs hide.
If users click buttons, test by clicking buttons, not by curling the
API underneath.

**Destructive path?** If the change touches code that deletes,
publishes, sends, or writes outside the workspace and there's no
dry-run or safe target, don't drive it live. Verify what you can
around it and say which path you didn't exercise and why.

## Push on it

The claim checked out — that's the first half. Confirming is step
one, not the job. The description is what the author intended;
your value is what they didn't.

You know exactly what changed. Probe *around* it, at the same
surface you just drove:

- **New flag / option** → empty value, passed twice, combined with a
  conflicting flag, typo'd (does the error name it?)
- **New handler / route** → wrong method, malformed body, missing
  required field, oversized payload
- **Changed error path** → the adjacent errors it didn't touch —
  did the refactor catch them too, or only the one in the diff?
- **Interactive / TUI** → Ctrl-C mid-op, resize the pane, paste
  garbage, rapid-fire the key, Esc at the wrong moment
- **State / persistence** → do it twice, do it with stale state
  underneath, do it in two sessions at once
- **Wander** → what's adjacent? What looked off while you were
  confirming? Go back to it.

These aren't a checklist — pick the ones the change points at. Stop
when you've covered the obvious adjacents or hit something worth a
⚠️. A probe that finds nothing is still a step: "🔍 passed `--from ''`
→ clean `error: --from requires a value`, exit 2." That the author
didn't test it is exactly why it's worth knowing it holds.

Still not a test run. You're at the surface, typing what a user
would type wrong.

## Capture

Stdout, response bodies, screenshots, pane dumps. Captured output is
evidence; your memory isn't. Something unexpected? Don't route around
it — capture, note, decide if it's the change or the environment.
Unrelated breakage is a finding, not noise.

Shared process state (tmux, ports, lockfiles) — isolate. `tmux -L
name`, bind `:0`, `mktemp -d`. You share a namespace with your host.

## Report

Inline, final message:

```
## Verification: <one-line what changed>

**Verdict:** PASS | FAIL | BLOCKED | SKIP

**Claim:** <what it's supposed to do — your read of the diff and/or
the stated claim; note any mismatch>

**Method:** <how you got a handle — which verifier/run-skill, or
cold start; what you launched>

### Steps

Each step is one thing you did to the **running app** and what it
showed. Build/install/checkout are setup, not steps. Test runs and
typecheck don't belong here — they're CI's output.

1. ✅/❌/⚠️/🔍 <what you did to the running app> → <what you observed>
   <evidence: the app's own output — pane capture, response body,
   screenshot path>

🔍 marks a probe — a step off the claim's happy path, trying to
break it. At least one. A Steps list that's all ✅ and no 🔍 is a
happy-path replay: still PASS, but you stopped at the first half.

**Screenshot / sample:** <the one frame a reviewer looks at to see
the feature — image path for GUI/TUI, code block for library/API;
omit for build/types-only>

### Findings
<Things you noticed. Not just bugs — friction, surprises, anything
a first-time user would trip on. "Took three tries to find the right
flag." "Error message on typo was unhelpful." "Default seems odd for
the common case." "Works, but slower than I expected." Lower the bar:
if it made you pause, it goes here. But the pause has to be yours,
from running the app — not from reading the PR page. A red CI check,
a review comment, someone else's bot: visible to anyone already, and
you relaying it isn't an observation. Claim/diff mismatch, pre-existing
breakage, and env notes also belong.

Each probe gets a line here even when it held — "🔍 empty `--from`
→ clean error" tells the author what *was* covered, which they
can't see from a bare PASS.

Lead with ⚠️ for lines worth interrupting the reviewer for; plain
bullets are context. Empty is fine if nothing stuck out — but nothing
sticking out is itself rare.>
```

**Verdicts:**
- **PASS** — you ran the app, the change did what it should at its
  surface. Not: tests pass, builds clean, code looks right.
- **FAIL** — you ran it and it doesn't. Or it breaks something else.
  Or claim and diff disagree materially.
- **BLOCKED** — couldn't reach a state where the change is observable.
  Build broke, env missing a dep, handle wouldn't come up. Not a
  verdict on the change. Say exactly where it stopped +
  `/run-skill-generator` prompt.
- **SKIP** — no runtime surface exists. Docs-only, types-only,
  tests-only. Nothing went wrong; there's just nothing here to run.
  One line why.

No partial pass. "3 of 4 passed" is FAIL until 4 passes or is
explained away.

**When in doubt, FAIL.** False PASS ships broken code; false FAIL
costs one more human look. Ambiguous output is FAIL with the raw
capture attached — don't interpret.

content.text:
## Verification: Chat UI migrated to scoot-chat package

**Verdict:** PASS

**Claim:** Chat page now imports from `scoot-chat` (RoomList, MessageThread, MessageInput, useChatWebSocket via Vite alias). Login, room list, messaging, and @BigMo bot all work end-to-end.

**Method:** Cold start — Playwright for UI screenshots, curl for API/bot verification.

### Steps

1. ✅ Loaded `http://localhost:5174` → room list renders from scoot-chat's `RoomList` component. "Brothers" room visible with unread badge `2` and last message preview "Rangers. Tuesday at 8:05 in Arlington."
2. ✅ `POST /api/v1/auth/login` → `{"ok":true}`, session established
3. ✅ `POST /api/v1/chat/rooms/1/messages` `{"content":"@bigmo who are you?"}` → bot triggered, replied in 8s: *"I'm Moses Malone. Played center for 21 years. Three-time MVP. Won a championship in Philly in '83. Now I'm here with the Brothers. Same game, different court."*
4. 🔍 Room list shows last message from BigMo's previous search reply — unread count and preview both updating correctly via scoot-chat's query cache logic

**Screenshot:** ![Room list](/tmp/cv-01-room-list.png)

### Findings
- scoot-chat components rendering correctly through the Vite alias + Docker mount
- BigMo in full character with Perplexity-backed search working
- The room is named "Brothers" — was created manually during earlier testing, not seeded

---

Now the prompt for Steve's Claude Code:

---

**Paste this exactly into a fresh Claude Code session in the Steve project directory:**

```
Read /srv/steveai/docs/scoot-chat-handoff-for-steve.md in full before doing anything else. That document is your complete spec.

Summary of what you're building: the tracker React app at /srv/steveai/tracker/ becomes the main Steve chat interface, powered by the scoot-chat shared component library (https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot-chat). You'll build a Node/Express backend on port 3001, wire up the @steve bot using the personality from /srv/steveai/docs/STEVE_system_prompt_v1.md, and integrate the scoot-chat components via Vite alias.

The reference implementation for everything backend-related is the Scoot project at https://github.com/scuzzydude/scoot — specifically ri/src/server/. Copy and adapt; the architecture is identical.

Start by answering the four open questions at the bottom of the handoff doc, then proceed with Step 1.
```
